"I found this weird behavior in Chrome where if I quickly hover my mouse outside of the tab strip and then immediately go back to it, the delay for showing the tab preview resets to 0. This is happening on my latest nightly build (126.0a1) on Windows 10x64, macOS 14, and Ubuntu 22 - but not on any other platforms I tested. The weird thing is that if I accidentally leave the tab strip and then come back, Chrome will keep the delay at 0 even after I've left and re-entered the strip. This doesn't seem like a regression since this is actually an enhancement that was added in January 2024 - but it's definitely not what I expected! One thing that might be useful about this behavior is that when you accidentally leave the tab strip, Chrome will keep the delay at 0 until you intentionally hover out of the strip again. This could come in handy if you have a high-resolution screen and small tabs, or if you're using a trackpad - I can think of a few scenarios where this would be really useful!"

Hey team, I just found a pretty serious issue on Firefox profile accounts.firefox.com. When I tried to upload an SVG file with some malicious code inside (don't worry, it's not actually harmful, just a proof-of-concept), I was able to store an XSS attack that wasn't blocked by the Content Security Policy (CSP). The weird thing is that even though the CSP caught the attack and prevented it from running initially, the file itself got stored on the server - which means an attacker could potentially exploit this vulnerability to attack your servers directly. Here's how I reproduced the issue:

1. Opened Firefox and logged into my profile
2. Clicked "Change Image Profile" and attached the SVG file with the malicious code (xss.svg)
3. Right-clicked on the uploaded image, selected "Inspect Element", and previewed it in a new tab
4. The XSS attack executed and popped up an alert box showing the domain name

But here's the thing - even though the attack got blocked by the CSP initially, if I saved the image and then right-clicked on it again to open it in a new tab, the XSS attack was still stored on the server. This is pretty concerning because an attacker could potentially exploit this vulnerability to launch a direct attack on your servers.

So, my expected result would be that the XSS attack gets filtered out by the CSP and doesn't get stored on the server - but unfortunately, it looks like there's a window of opportunity for an attacker to exploit this issue if they can figure out how to bypass the initial filter.

Hey team, I've got a weird one to share. So, when you upload an SVG file with some malicious code in Firefox on accounts.firefox.com, it looks like it allows for an unrestricted upload - which is bad news! But here's the thing: because of Content Security Policy (CSP), the Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) payload I created gets blocked. The weird part is that even though it doesn't work when you try to execute it, the file itself gets stored on the server storage. That means if an attacker can get their hands on this file, they could potentially bypass the filter and attack your server directly - which is super concerning! 

Let me walk you through how I discovered this issue:

* Open Firefox
* Click on my profile account
* Change my image profile
* Attach the SVG file I created (xss.svg)
* Right-click on it and inspect the element
* Preview the image in a new tab - and that's when the XSS payload should execute, but since CSP is enabled, it gets blocked
* If you don't believe me, just click the link and reload the page - or try to open it in a new tab - and it won't work. But here's the thing: if an attacker can somehow get this file stored on your server, they could still exploit it!

So, what I expected was that the XSS payload would pop up when I opened the image in a new tab, but because CSP is enabled, it got filtered out. Instead, we've got a situation where the malicious file gets stored on your server - which is super concerning!

As you can see, while the CSP blocked the immediate execution of the XSS attack, the fact that the malicious file gets stored on the server means it's still a potential vulnerability waiting to be exploited. Here's the rewritten bug report in a natural, conversational tone:

So, I found this pretty cool (and not-so-cool) thing about how Firefox handles private cache preloading using Fetch. Essentially, if you get the right combination of circumstances, you can use Fetch to preload the browser's private cache and then poison normal navigation. This opens up a whole range of attacks that were previously thought to be unexploitable - yeah, not cool at all!

So, here's what happens: because the browser shares its private cache between Fetch and normal navigation based on the URI and origin context, an attacker can pre-request resources to poison the cache by adding headers to the Fetch request. Then, when you navigate to the page, it triggers the attack.

To make this work, you need two things:

* The Fetch request and response have to be cacheable under normal circumstances (usually a GET method with 301/308 status code or explicit caching headers). And, of course, the response has to be implicitly or explicitly cacheable.
* The target endpoint has to pass a pre-flight check because adding extra headers requires CORS.

Oh, and here's an interesting side note: even if the actual response isn't available for Fetch, the browser cache can still get updated successfully if the pre-flight passes - as long as it doesn't have an Access-Control-Allow-Origin header!

Recommendation-wise, I think it'd be a good idea to add more info to the cache key so that requests from Fetch and normal navigation are separate.

user agent: mozilla/5.0 (windows nt 10.0; win64; x64; rv:109.0) gecko/20100101 firefox/109.0

steps to reproduce:

# tl;dr
if the right set of circumstances are present, fetch can be used to pre-load the browser private cache, and poison normal navigation. using this approach, a range of attacks that were previously thought to be unexploitable, can be unlocked and made practical. hurrah!

# blah blah blah
the reason that this works is because the browser shares the private cache between fetch and normal navigation, with the cache key being based upon the uri and originating context. however, because fetch is able to add headers to the request that can vary the response (but which are not included in the cache key), an attacker can pre-request resources to poison the private cache, then force the browser to navigate to the page, which activates the attack.

to make this work requires two things:

- the fetch request and response must be cacheable under normal circumstances by the browser. typically, a get method, and the response must be implicitly cacheable (301/308 status code, and last-modified header), or explicitly cacheable (cache-control, pragma and expires headers).

- the target endpoint must pass a pre-flight check, as adding additional headers to a fetch request requires cors. 

as an interesting observation, the response does not always have to be made available to fetch for the browser cache to be updated. for example, if the pre-flight passes, but the actual response does not have an access-control-allow-origin header, then it will be blocked due to cors. but even so, the cache will sometimes still be updated successfully.


# recommendations
it is recommended that additional information is added to the cache key, so that requests made by fetch and the normal navigation are separated.

Here's the rewritten bug report:

I've attached a file called rendertest.html that shows an issue with Firefox (specifically version 132.0) - it's really annoying and I'm hoping someone can help me fix it! 

So, here's what's going on: when you have a bunch of elements inside a container stage, where the overflow is hidden, and each element is positioned next to another at a small distance, if you add pointer event listeners that apply dragging actions over the stage surface, all the elements should move in sync. And they do - except for those outside the viewport that come into view from the hidden overflow area. 

In Firefox 132.0, there's this weird graphical delay when these elements come into view, like the rendering of their translation is being delayed or something. It's really noticeable if you compare it to the same test in Chrome (version 131). I've even made a video demo that shows this issue - it's at http://163.172.222.229/temp/v.mp4 (note: the file will be taken down once this case is resolved).

I did some digging and found out that this issue started happening around Firefox version 124.0, but in version 123.0 everything was working fine. So, I'm guessing it's something to do with an optimization or two somewhere that's causing these issues.

In my experience, when browsers optimize performance, they should still stick to the rules of how elements should behave according to web standards. In this case, it seems like the optimisation is breaking the logic of how similar elements should behave in a consistent way. I'm hoping someone can help me resolve this issue within a month - thanks!

Here's a rewritten version of the bug report in a natural, conversational tone:

I created an attachment called "noresetidleoneventbydefault.firefox112.diff". To reproduce this issue, I just ran Firefox with a bunch of extensions and multiple windows and tabs open. What happened was that Firefox became super slow - not what I expected at all!

After digging deeper using the Firefox profiler, I found some interesting stuff. It turns out that there's an optional parameter called "resetidleonevent" in the constructor of the "EventManager" class, which is set to true by default (https://hg.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/file/a8939ff5236dad956af827235ceed7104e5e92c2/toolkit/components/extensions/extensioncommon.jsm#l2271). When this parameter is true, it triggers some expensive machinery that costs a lot of CPU resources and time. In fact, handling an event becomes roughly twice as costly when "resetidleonevent" is set to true.

To be honest, I haven't found any justification for this extra cost. So, I think we should make "resetidleonevent" false by default and only enable it explicitly where necessary. This patch makes that happen:

--- a/toolkit/components/extensions/extensioncommon.jsm
+++ b/toolkit/components/extensions/extensioncommon.jsm
@@ -2268,7 +2268,7 @@
       register,
       extensionapi,
       inputhandling = false,
-      resetidleonevent = true,
+      resetidleonevent = false,
     } = params;
     this.context = context;
     this.module = module;

With this patch, Firefox uses less CPU resources and is generally more responsive - even with a bunch of extensions and windows open.

The cost of having "resetidleonevent" set to true is that it triggers some unnecessary code. Specifically, whenever an event listener is called, it sends a cross-process message named "background-script-reset-idle" (https://hg.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/file/a8939ff5236dad956af827235ceed7104e5e92c2/toolkit/components/extensions/extensioncommon.jsm#l2603). This code is repeated every time an event listener is called, which adds up to a lot of overhead.

In fact, there are around 110 places in the Mozilla-central code where "new EventManager" is called (as of Firefox 112), and only one of them provides this optional parameter explicitly. So, almost all event handlers are affected by this performance bug.

The intention behind the "resetidleonevent" feature seems to be to prevent suspending background pages of extensions when they've recently received an event listener call. However, if that's the case, then the current implementation is actually consuming more CPU resources than would be saved by suspending those pages. Not to mention, it consumes resources even if there's no background page or CPU consumption! A more efficient implementation could trade off timing accuracy for efficiency.

For example, we could periodically check whether each extension's background page should be suspended (based on the preference "extensions.background.idle.timeout", e.g., every 30 seconds). If that flag is set to true, we'd clear it. If it's false, then no one has set it to true since the last check. Then, we'd call the suspend code. Whenever an event handler is called for that extension, we'd set the "recentlyactive" flag to true, which would prevent running the suspend code on the next periodic check.

This implementation would add a small penalty of just one "extension.recentlyactive = true" call per event handler call, instead of the current overhead of inter-process communication, JSON serialization and deserialization, two context switches, resolving the "@mozilla.org/timer;1" interface, and so on. Note that in the current implementation, there's one timer per extension (e.g., 20 timers for a user with 20 extensions), but in this suggested implementation, there would be only 1 timer.

So, I think we should disable the inefficient code that decreases performance until its effects have been found to be more advantageous than without enabling it.
text:
created attachment 9330995
noresetidleoneventbydefault.firefox112.diff

steps to reproduce:

i ran firefox with a few extensions and a lot of windows and tabs.


actual results:

i found that firefox was slow.


expected results:

firefox should have been fast.


i have analyzed this further using the firefox profiler and found the following:

the constructor of class "eventmanager" has an undocumented optional parameter "resetidleonevent" which is set to true by default. (https://hg.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/file/a8939ff5236dad956af827235ceed7104e5e92c2/toolkit/components/extensions/extensioncommon.jsm#l2271)

if resetidleonevent==true, then, whenever an event happens, a very expensive machinery is called. the machinery is expensive in terms of cpu resources and hence user time and battery charge. it includes at least 2 additional context switches per event. the cpu cost of handling an event seems to be roughly doubled if resetidleonevent==true than if resetidleonevent==false.

the increase in cpu expense does not seem to be justified. at least, i have not found any justification. therefore, i believe that resetidleonevent should be false by default. any code which needs this feature should ask for it explicitly.

this can be accomplished with this patch:

--- a/toolkit/components/extensions/extensioncommon.jsm
+++ b/toolkit/components/extensions/extensioncommon.jsm
@@ -2268,7 +2268,7 @@
       register,
       extensionapi,
       inputhandling = false,
-      resetidleonevent = true,
+      resetidleonevent = false,
     } = params;
     this.context = context;
     this.module = module;

with this patch, firefox consumes less cpu resources and has less latency, it is more "snappy", even with a few extensions and a lot of windows and tabs.



the cost of resetidleonevent==true
==================================

if resetidleonevent==true, then whenever an event listener is called, then also a cross-process message named "background-script-reset-idle" is emitted (from the main process to the extension process) (https://hg.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/file/a8939ff5236dad956af827235ceed7104e5e92c2/toolkit/components/extensions/extensioncommon.jsm#l2603).

once the extension process receives the message, the function "resetbackgroundidle" is called (https://hg.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/file/a8939ff5236dad956af827235ceed7104e5e92c2/toolkit/components/extensions/parent/ext-backgroundpage.js#l397).
this function cancels an existing "backgroundtimer" and then resolves the "@mozilla.org/timer;1" interface and then instantiates a new instance of such a timer. it also computes data for telemetry. it barely does any useful work, except for the very unlikely case that backgroundstate of the extension is background_state.suspending, in which case the a "background-script-suspend-canceled" message is emitted.

this code, including the cross-process context switches, is repeated every time when an event listener is called.

there are about 110 call sites of "new eventmanager" in the current mozilla-central code (as of firefox 112), and only 1 of them provides the "resetidleonevent" optional parameter explicitly. this means that almost all event handlers are affected by this performance bug.



the intention of the resetidleonevent feature seems to be to prevent suspending background pages of extensions in case they have recently received any event listener calls, and to otherwise permit suspending background pages (i.e. for saving cpu resources). however, if this is the intention, then the current implementation seems to consume more considerably more cpu resources than would be saved by suspending background pages. also, if consumes cpu resources even if there was no background page or no cpu consumption by the background page. a more efficient implementation could trade suspend timing accuracy for efficiency, as follows:

periodically (as defined in preference "extensions.background.idle.timeout", e.g. every 30 seconds), for each extension, it would be checked whether its background page shall be suspended. this check checks a flag "recentlyactive". if that flag was set to true, the check sets it to false. if that flag was false, then between the last check and now, no one has set it to true. then, the check calls the suspend code. whenever an event handler is called for that extension, the "recentlyactive" flag is set to true, which will prevent running the suspend code upon the next periodic check.

such an implementation would call the suspend code maybe not immediately 30 seconds after the last action, but up to another period length (another 30 seconds) later. however, such an implementation would add a penalty of only one "extension.recentlyactive = true" call per event handler call, instead of adding the penalty of inter-process communication, json serialization and deserialization, 2 context switches, resolving the "@mozilla.org/timer;1" interface, and so on, per event handler call. note that in the current implementation, in addition to the penalty described above, there is one timer per extension (e.g. there will be 20 timers if the user has 20 extensions), but in the suggested implementation, there would be only 1 timer.


therefore, i believe that the current inefficient code which decreases performance shall only be enabled (again) when its effects on performance have been found to be more advantageous than without enabling it. until then, it should be disabled (which the above patch "resetidleonevent = false" achieves partially).

Then it passes that `mcertificate` to sha256_update_native and boom! access violation read.

When I looked at the rtccertificate.cpp code, it seems like this function is being called from another thread (there's a comment saying "run heavy crypto ops on the thread pool, off the original thread"). 

So I added some printf statements to print the address of `mcertificate` in rtccertificate!signcertificate and peerconnectionimpl::setcertificate. When I call new rtcpeerconnection(), both addresses match, but when I repeatedly visit this test case in a loop, the `mcertificate` address is out of sync. It looks like rtccertificate is generating too many `mcertificates`, freeing older ones, and then peerconnectionimpl::setcertificate is still taking the old (freed) `mcertificate` address instead of the fresh one - causing a use-after-free.

## Test Results:
I tested this on Firefox 113.0a1 (2023-04-01) (32-bit) and Firefox 111.0.1 (32-bit), both with the same results.

## Steps to Reproduce:
1. Open up Firefox 32-bit
2. Visit attached testcase1.html on an HTTPS site
3. Leave it running for a bit until the tab crashes
4. Analyze the crash dump"

Here's a rewritten version of the bug report in a natural, conversational tone:

So, here's what happened: Amazon Trust Services (ATS) has two intermediate certificates that aren't showing up on our audit reports, but they are actually listed in Mozilla's Root Program. This is causing an Audit Letter Validation (ALV) check to fail. 

To give some background, we created these intermediate certificates back in October 2015. When we reviewed them later that year, we found two issues and decided to reissue corrected versions. The problem was that the validity period of those old certificates had already expired, and the "crl" field was listed as "caissuers" instead of the correct "crt". We made some changes to our system to prevent this from happening again in the future, and by December 2015, we had deleted those old certificates and issued new ones.

So, how did we find out about this issue? Well, during a review of our CA communications in January 2020, we noticed that our audit reports were missing some certificates. We started looking into it and realized that there was a mismatch between the number of ALV errors and the number of certificates listed in CCADB (Mozilla's Root Program). That's when we started investigating and discussing with our auditors about what to do.

Here's a timeline of what happened:

* January 30, 2020: We reached out to Mozilla for clarification on resolving some formatting errors during ALV.
* January 30, 2020: We contacted our audit team about the ALV failures.
* January 31, 2020: We identified the mismatch and started investigating. We also began discussing with our auditors what to do about it.
* February 12, 2020: We determined with our auditors that it wouldn't be appropriate to add these certificates to the audit reports.
* April 13, 2021: We updated our audit reports in CCADB and noted that there were existing ALV errors that needed to be discussed at our next CA/B Forum and Trust Store Policy meeting on April 30, 2021.
* April 30, 2021: We finalized answers for the April 2021 CA communications, including a plan for resolving ALV issues.

By the way, we actually deleted those old certificates back in December 2015, so we're not still using them. 

Now, about the problematic certificates:

We have two certificates that weren't listed on our audit reports: https://crt.sh/?sha256=80dd9e3497f354e30b8acf39d046dd4f5a618f7889236eb34f78d54d15cd6a50 and https://crt.sh/?sha256=e39d3ed886e5a3af26b9d6ab608028bc6fbc52e599cb323da7e9e775b530337c.

And finally, about how we made this mistake: We should have opened a ticket with Mozilla in February 2020 to get clarity on the new policy requiring all intermediate certificates in Mozilla's Root Program to be listed in every CA's annual audit report. 

To resolve this situation and prevent it from happening again, we're going to revoke those two intermediate certificates by June 30, 2021. We also have a monthly review meeting where we review changes to the CA/B Forum and Trust Store Policy, so we'll start including CCADB summary page and CRT.SH Mozilla disclosures in that meeting as well.

"I'm running Firefox 120 on my Raspberry Pi OS (based on Debian Bookworm) with Wayfire as the Wayland compositor, and I just upgraded from Firefox 119 to get the new features. But sadly, it looks like something went wrong because now my WebGL performance is super slow - I've got a major drop in frame rates. After doing some digging, I found that the issue started with a specific commit (https://phabricator.services.mozilla.com/d189920) related to bug 1856582. It seems that this change messed up the `record_warning` and `record_error` functions by removing newlines from their output. Now, when WebGL errors occur, they're getting stuck on the same line as the preceding warning message, which is causing my parser to choke and disable dmabufs. I've attached my Firefox support info (about:support) before and after the breakage - you can see the problem in the gfx failure log."

created attachment 9366096
support_bad

steps to reproduce:

- running on raspberry pi os bookworm, based on debian bookworm, using the wayfire wayland compositor.
- upgraded from firefox 119 to 120



actual results:

upgrading from firefox 119 to 120 breaks dmabufs, which drastically reduces performance on webgl.  i did a git bisect and the problem is a regression in from https://phabricator.services.mozilla.com/d189920 from bug 1856582.

digging into it, the problem is because that commit changes `record_warning` and `record_error` and removes the newline after the content of the warning/error.  since the warning/error messages themselves don't contain newlines, this means that the next line in glxtest output gets appended on the same line as the warning/error.  in my case this means the `drm_renderdevice` line gets glued onto the preceding warning and so the parser doesn't see it and disables dmabufs.

i've attached my `about:support` from the commit before and after the breakage, the problem can be seen in the gfx failure log.

"Hey, I've got a bug report to share with you! So, we're researchers who care about online privacy, and we've found a weird issue in Firefox (specifically version 92.0.1). It seems that when you use private browsing mode and fetch some cross-origin resources, the browser can cache those requests in a way that's kinda sneaky. What's even worse is that once you close and reopen the private window, those cached requests just keep on persisting! For example, if someone encoded a unique identifier into those CORS requests, they could use it to track your activity across different private browsing sessions. To reproduce this bug, all you need to do is open a website in private mode that fetches some cross-origin resources (make sure the 'Access-Control-Max-Age' header is set), close and reopen the window, and then check if those cached requests are still there. Trust me, it's weird - we even attached some screenshots to show you what we mean!"
text:
created attachment 9248146
screenshots.zip

user agent: mozilla/5.0 (macintosh; intel mac os x 10_14_6) applewebkit/537.36 (khtml, like gecko) chrome/95.0.4638.54 safari/537.36

steps to reproduce:

we are privacy researchers evaluating the privacy implications of browser caching mechanisms. we have identified a bug in firefox 92.0.1 that presents a privacy threat to users as it can be used to re-identify them across private browsing sessions. specifically, we have found that cached options requests for cors resources persist after closing a private browsing window and opening a new one. in practice, attackers can encode a unique  identifier into a series of cors requests, and use it to track a user across private browsing sessions.

steps to reproduce:

open a website (in private browsing) which fetches a cross-origin resource, such that the 'access-control-max-age' header in response is set to a non-zero value. 

close and re-open the window and access the same url, and check if the options request is still cached

in the attached screenshot, we can see that a preflight request was not triggered for request 31, even after closing and re-opening the private window.

just like the example above, an user identifier can be encoded as a series of bits such that: 

cors cache hit=1
cors cache miss=0

this identifier would be able to persist across private browsing sessions, which presents a potential privacy vulnerability


actual results:

on closing the private browsing window and re-opening it, the cors preflight cache persists and the cross-origin resource is directly fetched without a preflight request. in essence, the cache is only cleared when the browser is terminated.



expected results:

on closing the private browsing window, the cors preflight cache should have been erased. in other words, when the window is reopened and the website is visited, there should be a preflight request triggered for resource 31 in the above screenshot.

cbh_unenrollment.gif

**[affected versions]:**
- firefox nightly 111.0a1 (build id 20230212085204)

**[prerequisites]:**
- have a browser profile enrolled in the cbh nimbus experiment.

**[steps to reproduce]:**
1. open the browser with the profile from prerequisites.
2. navigate to a website with cookie banners (eg. cnn.com).
3. click on the "reject requests" button.
4. navigate to the about:studies page and observe the list of studies.

**[expected result]:**
- the cookie banner is dismissed and the client remains enrolled in the experiment.

**[actual result]:**
- the cookie banner remains displayed and the user is unenrolled from the experiment.

**[notes]:**
- the cause of this issue is that clicking the "reject requests" button will flip the "cookiebanners.service.detectonly" preference to "false". since this preference has its value modified by the experiment, flipping it causes the user to unenroll and all other preferences to reset to their default values.
- unenrollment by modifying an experiment's preference is the expected behavior for nimbus experiments

It started when our validation team detected an anomaly in one of the certificates we issued. Specifically, they noticed that one of the certificates had an 'org name' included in the O field without proper approval from our validation agents.

After investigating further, we found that there were two main issues causing this problem. The first was a UI issue where our validation staff could select more than just the company name while validating information. This led to some mis-issued certificates with DBAs (distinguished business associates) included in the certificate without approval.

The second issue was related to an improved validation proxy that added shortened company names to EV certificates as DBAs before they were passed on to our CA for issuance. We fixed the primary issue by updating the code to only include approved DBAs and not cached validation information.

I've attached a screenshot of the UI before and after the changes for reference. And as requested, I'm providing the complete certificate data for the problematic certificates in an attached spreadsheet."

"I'm reporting an issue with Firefox 101 on Windows 10. When I launch a new window using remote debugging, the initial tab doesn't seem to register as a target and isn't returned by `target.gettargets`. This is only happening on Windows - my repro environment is set up with Windows 10 and Firefox 101.

To reproduce this issue, follow these steps:

1. Start Firefox 101 with remote debugging enabled using the command: `start 'c:\program files (x86)\mozilla firefox\firefox.exe' 'about:blank', '--remote-debugging-port=51959'`.
2. Connect to the launched instance and run a simple script that issues the `target.gettargets` message (check out this gist for the script: https://gist.github.com/tbiethman/9add1e991efbecdbe3e6eddec9fb4b46).

What happens instead is that the `target.gettargets` message resolves with no found targets. The output looks like this:

```
found targets
{ targetinfos: [] }
```

But what I expect to see is the initial tab target returned, with an output like:

```
found targets
{
  targetinfos: [{
    targetid: 'xxxxxxx-xxxxxx-xxxxxxx',
    type: 'page',
    title: '',
    url: 'about:blank',
    attached: false,
    browsercontextid: null,
  }]
}
```

This is what happens when I run the same steps against Firefox 100, and also on the Mac version of Firefox 101 - the initial tab target returns as expected.

A couple more data points that might be relevant:

* If I spawn another window from the initial one (using Ctrl+N), the script starts returning targets after subsequent executions.
* If I launch the initial window in troubleshooting mode (-safe-mode option), the tab does return as a target for the initial window.
* I thought maybe this was related to how Firefox 101 handles 'about:blank', but launching to an actual location (like https://www.mozilla.org) still results in no targets being returned.

If there's anything else you'd like me to provide or if you think this behavior is now expected, please let me know!"

I've been noticing a weird issue with the card preview feature in Firefox - specifically, when I have multiple tabs open and I hover my mouse over one of them that's not currently active. The problem is that if I move my cursor around within that tab (even just hovering over the close button or title icon), the preview window suddenly disappears and then reappears as soon as I'm back to hovering over the same spot where it originally disappeared. This happens consistently across both Windows 11 and Ubuntu 22.04, but not on Mac OS 13.6. To reproduce this issue, you'll need to have the card preview enabled (just toggle that setting in about:config), then follow these steps: open two tabs with different websites, hover over one of them that's not currently active, and move your cursor around within that tab without switching focus away from it. The expected behavior is that the preview window stays visible as long as I'm still hovering over that same tab - but what actually happens is it disappears and then reappears as I move my mouse around. This issue has been happening on both the latest nightly build (124.0a1, 2024-01-25) and an older version from January 13th, so it doesn't seem to be a regression or anything that just started recently.

Here's a rewritten version of the bug report in a natural, conversational tone:

"I recently discovered a weird issue with Firefox that I thought was worth reporting. Essentially, if a website is able to control the favicons and titles of open tabs, it can create a situation where those tabs essentially become invisible to the user - meaning they don't have any visual contrast from one another. This means the user won't be able to see the tabs they've opened, which could be pretty problematic.

To demonstrate this issue, I created a simple JSFiddle (https://jsfiddle.net/pcgmwy25/) that you can use to reproduce it - just pop up any new windows or tabs and you'll see what I mean. The key is that at some point, Firefox stops rendering the "close" button for these invisible tabs.

The attached screenshot (mozilla-invisible-tabs.png) shows exactly what's going on - as soon as a website opens an invisible tab, the "+" button to open new tabs moves around because the tab isn't actually visible anymore. My expectation is that Firefox should always provide some kind of visual contrast between tabs, regardless of whether a website is controlling their favicons and titles or not."
text:
created attachment 9258752
mozilla-invisible-tabs.png

user agent: mozilla/5.0 (windows nt 10.0; win64; x64; rv:96.0) gecko/20100101 firefox/96.0

steps to reproduce:

firefox depends on favicons and titles to give open tabs visual contrast. these being under control of a website means they have the ability to create contrast-less tabs which are "invisible" to the user.

this very basic jsfiddle demonstrates it. it does depend on the number of tabs opened vs the width of the window, because at some point firefox stops rending the "close" button.

https://jsfiddle.net/pcgmwy25/

popups must be accepted for this specific example to work, but an attack would not specifically require such a thing, as they could be links that open in a new tab with these features, and the user "does this to themselves."


actual results:

the website is able to open tabs that the user can not see. this permits the website to continue to run scripts or perform other behavior in the background without the user's knowledge.

the attached screen shot demonstrates the issue pretty clearly. one visible change is the movement of the new tab "+" button.


expected results:

the tabs should have visual contrast independent of site controlled input.

However, when it's at the bottom, you can't access the section using the screen reader software...

Here's the rewritten bug report in a natural, conversational tone:

I created an attachment called `testcase.bundle.html` (you can find it as #9297325) and after launching the child popup window using `window.open`, I noticed something weird. If I run `window.open` again with the same `windowname`, the child window can repeatedly gain focus without requiring any user input - which is not supposed to happen! What's even crazier is that this allows the child window to overlap another popup, like a fullscreen notification toast.

It turns out that this issue started showing up after some specific changes were made in the code. According to the pushlog, it looks like the problem popped up around February 9th, and if you want to take a closer look at the exact commits that caused the issue, you can check out [this link](https://hg.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/pushloghtml?fromchange=566f81bfa373512b41c1a47962e21a06078d7bf8&tochange=89c5f958a3ac4795109acf1f9dff1c8026bb82fe). 

One of the commits that caught my attention was [this one](https://hg.mozilla.org/mozilla-central/rev/d3539dfe1b07c4937e084844999c515475e869bb), which mentions something about taking focus from `window.open` even if we're already active but not in the active window. So, I decided to roll back some code changes on `nsdocshell.cpp` and sure enough, when I re-ran `window.open`, the child window wouldn't be able to gain focus anymore.

To reproduce this issue, follow these steps:

1. Open up the attached `testcase.bundle.html` file on a Linux OS.
2. Click "Launch Stealfocuswindow".
3. Click "Launch RequestFullscreenWindow".
4. Click "Request Fullscreen" on the child popup window.
5. And that's when the fullscreen notification toast gets overlapped with the child window.

Tested this on the following platforms:

* Firefox 105.0.2 (64-bit) on Arch Linux (KDE X11)
* Firefox 105.0.2 (64-bit) on Arch Linux (KDE Wayland)
* Firefox 105.0.2 (64-bit) on Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS (Wayland)
* Firefox Nightly 107.0a1 (2022-10-05) (64-bit) on Arch Linux (KDE X11)
* Firefox Nightly 107.0a1 (2022-10-05) (64-bit) on Arch Linux (KDE Wayland)

So, I've got a weird issue with Firefox 91.0 on my Linux machine. I was using Firefox 80 and then upgraded to 81, which caused some problems (I filed bug #1667464). But after updating to v83.0, everything worked fine again.

Fast forward to when I updated from Firefox 90.0.2 to 91.0 - that's when things got weird. My Sony WH-XB900N headset stopped working properly with web conferencing software like 3CX and Webex during calls or meetings. It didn't matter if I gave permission for the mic temporarily or permanently; it just wouldn't work.

I've tried using a new profile and no extensions at all, but still, the problem persists. The speaker works fine, but the microphone is always muted! It's like it's stuck on mute or something.

I went back to Firefox 90.0.2, rebooted, and my headset worked again without any issues. So, I'm pretty sure this is a regression that happened between v90.0.2 and v91.0.

Here are the steps to reproduce:

1. Update from Firefox 90.0.2 to 91.0.
2. Reboot your machine.
3. Connect your headset (in my case, Sony WH-XB900N).
4. Go to a web conferencing site and grant access to the microphone.
5. Make a call or start a meeting - the mic should work, but it doesn't.

In short, upgrading from Firefox 90.0.2 to 91.0 broke the usability of my Bluetooth microphone. Lots of noise and the voice is unrecognizable. I hope someone can help me figure out what's going on!
